papergraph.title: MES in Pharmaceutical Manufacturing: Enabling the Future of Personalized Medicine
papergraph.citation: Sarita Santosh Dhage (2025) MES in Pharmaceutical Manufacturing: Enabling the Future of Personalized Medicine doi:10.37745/ejcsit.2013/vol13n28110
papergraph.venue: European journal of computer science and information technology
papergraph.abstract: Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) are emerging as critical enablers for the pharmaceutical industry's transition toward personalized medicine. As healthcare shifts from standardized treatments to individualized therapies tailored to patients' genetic profiles and specific needs, manufacturing processes must evolve accordingly. MES provides the technological foundation to address the unique challenges of personalized medicine production through streamlined batch management, flexible manufacturing capabilities, and real-time process control. These systems facilitate data-driven decision making by integrating research and development information with production processes, enabling continuous adaptation to patient-specific requirements. MES further ensures regulatory compliance through automated documentation and embedded quality control while supporting integration with diagnostic technologies to translate patient data into precise manufacturing parameters. Additionally, MES optimizes supply chain operations by improving material tracking, minimizing waste, and enabling efficient distribution of time-sensitive treatments. Together, these capabilities bridge the gap between the theoretical promise of personalized medicine and its practical implementation in patient care.

papergraph.title: From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing.
papergraph.citation: Lotte De Wever et al. (2025) From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing. doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2025.126271
papergraph.abstract: Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) is a widely used additive manufacturing technique for personalized pharmaceutical dosage forms but requires filaments with specific mechanical and physical properties, limiting formulation flexibility. To address this, pellets could be used as feedstock material for direct extrusion additive manufacturing (DEAM) where a conventional filament printing head is replaced by a single-screw extrusion unit. This study explores the potential of pellet-based DEAM through a design-of-experiments approach using a formulation unsuitable for FFF. Brittle filaments containing 60% anhydrous theophylline and 40% Soluplus® (m/m) were prepared via hot melt extrusion and shredded into pellets. The study evaluated the effects of process parameters during twin-screw extrusion (screw speed, barrel and die temperature) on various pellet characteristics, including content uniformity, API degradation, shape, size, flowability and friability. Die temperature exerted the most significant impact on the friability and visual quality of the pellets. A full factorial screening design evaluated the impact of four printing parameters (print temperature, infill, overlap and extrusion multiplier) on critical quality attributes of the 3D printed tablets, including tablet mass, content uniformity, drug degradation, mechanical properties, dissolution, dimensions, and porosity. Infill, extrusion multiplier, and overlap significantly influenced tablet mass. The tablet brittleness index was a reliable indication of the brittleness of the printlets, the tensile strength was mainly influenced by infill. Porosity was correlated with in vitro dissolution, both influenced by infill and extrusion multiplier. An increase in print temperature significantly enhanced the dissolution rate. All pellet runs and 3D printed tablets maintained excellent content uniformity without API degradation, regardless of the heating cycles. Pellet-based DEAM proved to be a promising approach for manufacturing oral solid dosage forms with versatile properties and fewer material constraints compared to FFF. The extrusion multiplier was identified as a new key process parameter, enabling personalized medicine by tailoring dose, porosity and dissolution profiles.

papergraph.title: 3D-Printed Pharmaceuticals: A Future Perspective on Personalized Drug Formulations
papergraph.citation: Priyanshi na (2025) 3D-Printed Pharmaceuticals: A Future Perspective on Personalized Drug Formulations doi:10.63345/ijrhs.net.v13.i2.4
papergraph.venue: International journal of research in humanities and social sciences
papergraph.abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) printing technology has emerged as a revolutionary tool in the field of pharmaceuticals, enabling the production of personalized drug formulations tailored to individual patient needs. This manuscript provides an in-depth exploration of the potential impact of 3D-printed pharmaceuticals on personalized medicine. It reviews the historical evolution of additive manufacturing in drug production, discusses the technological advancements up to 2018, and examines the current methodologies and statistical trends associated with 3D-printed drug formulations. Through a detailed literature review, methodological analysis, and statistical evaluation, this study highlights the benefits and challenges of incorporating 3D printing into pharmaceutical manufacturing. In addition, we present a pilot statistical analysis that compares key parameters in formulation consistency, dosage accuracy, and production scalability. Our findings suggest that 3D printing offers a promising alternative to conventional manufacturing methods, particularly for patients requiring customized therapies due to genetic variability, comorbidities, or other individualized needs. The manuscript concludes by emphasizing the need for further research and regulatory clarity to fully integrate 3D printing into mainstream pharmaceutical practice, ultimately enhancing therapeutic outcomes and patient compliance.

papergraph.title: Transformative roles of digital twins from drug discovery to continuous manufacturing: pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical perspectives
papergraph.citation: Ravi Maharjan et al. (2025) Transformative roles of digital twins from drug discovery to continuous manufacturing: pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical perspectives doi:10.1016/j.ijpx.2025.100409
papergraph.abstract: Digital Twins (DTs) represent a groundbreaking development tool in the pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical industries, providing virtual representations of physical entities, processes, or systems. This review investigates the transformative roles of DTs by examining their applications throughout the entire drug development lifecycle, from discovery to continuous manufacturing. By facilitating real-time monitoring and predictive analytics, DTs enhance operational efficiency, reduce costs, and improve product quality. Integration with advanced technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, further amplifies their capabilities, enabling sophisticated data analysis for preventive maintenance and manufacturing optimization. Despite these advantages, the implementation of DTs faces significant challenges, including data integration, model accuracy, and regulatory complexity. This review discusses these barriers while highlighting opportunities for innovation and automation through emerging technologies, including blockchain, nanotechnology, and dark factory. It also explores the potential of DTs to support personalized medicine through individualized treatments based on patient-specific data. Overall, this review highlights the current state, key challenges, and future perspectives of DT applications in pharmaceutical systems, emphasizing their potential to improve efficiency, quality, and patient outcomes.

papergraph.title: HyperADRs: A Hierarchical Hypergraph Framework for Drug-Gene-ADR Prediction
papergraph.citation: Ze Cai et al. (2025) HyperADRs: A Hierarchical Hypergraph Framework for Drug-Gene-ADR Prediction arXiv:2512.00137v1
papergraph.abstract: Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) are a major barrier to safe and effective pharmacotherapy and increasingly reflect higher order interactions between drugs, genetic background, and clinical phenotypes. Existing graph based approaches usually predict ADRs as properties of drugs or drug pairs, leaving the causal gene implicit and limiting their value for pharmacogenomic decision making. We introduce HyperADRs, a hierarchical hypergraph framework that predicts ADR risk at the level of drug-gene-ADR triads. Starting from curated pharmacogenomic annotations in PharmGKB and the pharmacogenomics subdatabase of DrugBank, we construct high confidence triplets and integrate them with auxiliary molecular, functional, and disease relations from precision-medicine-oriented knowledge graphs. Drugs, genes, and ADR concepts are embedded with modality appropriate pretrained models (UniMol, ESM2, SapBERT) and propagated through a hypergraph convolutional network. A FiLM based, query conditioned contrastive learning module learns context specific representations so that, given any two entities, the model retrieves the correct third entity against many candidates. To improve robustness and interpretability, we propose a nine category ADR macro system scheme that reduces large heterogeneous 'other' bins while aligning with organ system reasoning in clinical pharmacology. Across drug-, gene-, and ADR-held-out evaluations on PharmGKB, HyperADRs matches or exceeds strong baselines on ranking based metrics. When trained on PharmGKB and tested on unseen DrugBank triplets, HyperADRs maintains its ranking advantage, indicating that the learned representations capture transferable biological mechanisms and can support mechanistically grounded pharmacogenomic hypothesis generation.

evidence.title: Abstract NG08: Dissecting and quantifying pancreatic cancer plasticity using single-cell multiomics, lineage tracing and functional genomics reveals novel mediators of therapy resistance
evidence.citation: Arnav Mehta et al. (2024) Abstract NG08: Dissecting and quantifying pancreatic cancer plasticity using single-cell multiomics, lineage tracing and functional genomics reveals novel mediators of therapy resistance doi:10.1158/1538-7445.am2024-ng08
evidence.contribution: identification of novel transcription factors (TFs) that mediate epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity (EMP) and influence treatment resistance in PDAC, and a robust experimental and quantitative framework for studying tumor cell plasticity
evidence.limitation: To study the effect of plasticity in treatment resistance, we treated four barcoded cell lines with the first-line chemotherapy combination FOLFIRINOX (5-fluorouracil, oxaliplatin and SN-38, the active metabolite of irinotecan) or targeted therapy and performed scSeq yielding over 600,00 single-cell transcriptomic profiles.

causal_chains.trigger_evidence: We subsequently found Klf3 as an important regulator of PDAC proliferation independent of cell state. Importantly, we found that knockdown (KD) of several factors, such has Grhl2 and FoxA1, bias towards mesenchymal cell states, whereas KD of others such as Batf2, Snai1, Rel, Zeb1, Nr2f1 and Sox4 led to a bias towards epithelial cell states.

steps.claim: Bias towards mesenchymal cell states
steps.evidence: Importantly, we found that knockdown (KD) of several factors, such has Grhl2 and FoxA1, bias towards mesenchymal cell states

steps.claim: Bias towards epithelial cell states
steps.evidence: whereas KD of others such as Batf2, Snai1, Rel, Zeb1, Nr2f1 and Sox4 led to a bias towards epithelial cell states

causal_chains.outcome: Influence on cell state transitions
causal_chains.outcome_evidence: Interestingly, KD of several TFs influenced transition properties of cells by decreasing rates of EMT and MET across barcodes without biasing the overall clonal distribution towards a single cell state. This suggests a role for these TFs in enabling plasticity and facilitating state transitions.

evidence.title: From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing.
evidence.citation: Lotte De Wever et al. (2025) From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing. doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2025.126271
evidence.scenario: pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing (DEAM) for pharmaceutical dosage forms
evidence.benchmark: from print settings to printlet performance
evidence.contribution: Pellet-based DEAM is a promising approach for manufacturing oral solid dosage forms with versatile properties and fewer material constraints compared to FFF. The extrusion multiplier is identified as a new key process parameter, enabling personalized medicine by tailoring dose, porosity, and dissolution profiles.
evidence.limitation: A full factorial screening design evaluated the impact of four printing parameters (print temperature, infill, overlap and extrusion multiplier) on critical quality attributes of the 3D printed tablets, including tablet mass, content uniformity, drug degradation, mechanical properties, dissolution, dimensions, and porosity.

hypotheses.statement: If the competing claims about personalized medicine are evaluated under matched the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims conditions, then cancer progression, tumor evolution, drug resistance will separate which mechanism holds and identify the boundary condition the condition where the competing explanations diverge. The decisive test is constrained to classification performance under an explicit stress regime.
hypotheses.mechanism: Concrete mechanism chain: (1) method capability: Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) contributes through controlling spatial organization, material architecture, and process-structure-property relationships; (2) scenario target: in personalized medicine, the affected process is a measurable biological or clinical mechanism such as expression, pathway activation, response, adverse effect, or persistence; (3) measurable consequence: the bridge must produce a preregistered change in cancer progression, tumor evolution, drug resistance. The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is multi-omics, simulation. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter cancer progression, tumor evolution, drug resistance; if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc.
hypotheses.expected_value: Contradiction gaps are high-value because resolving them can update mechanism understanding, not merely fill a sparse method-scenario cell.
hypotheses.test_plan: Build a minimal benchmark for personalized medicine; compare Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) against canonical baselines; measure cancer progression, tumor evolution, drug resistance; include negative controls, ablations, and failure-mode analysis. Add a preregistered stress test for classification performance.

keynotes.title: miRNAs in drug response variability: potential utility as biomarkers for personalized medicine

keynote.title: miRNAs in drug response variability: potential utility as biomarkers for personalized medicine
keynote.core_problem: Interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy, due to differences in the levels of circulating miRNAs or genetic variants in miRNA genes.
contributions: Overview of studies that have investigated the relationship between miRNAs and drug response in different disease classes.
Consideration of miRNAs as potential predictive biomarkers for therapy response.
methods: Comprehensive search conducted from the international web database PubMed for papers published before January 2019.
assumptions: miRNAs modulate the expression of multiple protein-encoding genes at the post-transcriptional level.
Almost all physiological conditions are probably modulated by miRNAs, including pharmacological response.

gap_signals.text: Full experimental and observational data, specific methods, and detailed limitations are not provided in the abstract.

important_claims.claim: Differences in the levels of circulating miRNAs or genetic variants in miRNA genes can contribute to interindividual variability in drug response.
important_claims.evidence: Indeed, acting on the regulation of numerous genes involved in the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of drugs, differences in the levels of circulating miRNAs or genetic variants in the sequences of the miRNA genes can contribute to interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy.

causal_chains.trigger: Differences in miRNA levels or genetic variants

steps.claim: Regulation of numerous genes involved in the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of drugs is affected.
steps.evidence: Acting on the regulation of numerous genes involved in the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of drugs, differences in the levels of circulating miRNAs or genetic variants in the sequences of the miRNA genes can contribute to interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy.

causal_chains.outcome: Interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy.
interventions: Measurement of circulating miRNA levels or genetic variants in miRNA genes

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a comprehensive overview of the current understanding of miRNAs' role in drug response variability and their potential as biomarkers, which can guide future research in personalized medicine and biomarker development.

keynotes.title: Advancing omics technologies in acute respiratory distress syndrome: paving the way for personalized medicine

keynote.title: Advancing omics technologies in acute respiratory distress syndrome: paving the way for personalized medicine
keynote.core_problem: The heterogeneous nature of ARDS, caused by diverse etiologies, poses considerable challenges to accurate diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis, and conventional methods often fail to elucidate the pathophysiology of ARDS, thus limiting therapeutic efficacy.
contributions: Providing deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms through omics technologies, including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, lipidomics, and epigenomics.
Identifying potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers for ARDS.
methods: Genomic studies to identify genetic variants associated with ARDS susceptibility.
Transcriptomic and proteomic analyses to reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis.
Metabolomic analysis to highlight biomarkers for severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction.
Lipidomic analysis to uncover disruptions in lipid metabolism and key lipid species as biomarkers for severity and outcomes.
Epigenomic studies to explore DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs and their role in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.
experiments_or_evidence: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants such as polymorphisms in genes encoding angiotensin-converting enzyme, surfactant proteins, toll-like receptor 4, interleukin-6, Fas/FasL, and vascular endothelial growth factor.
Transcriptomic and proteomic studies reveal dysregulated inflammatory signaling, epithelial and endothelial barrier dysfunction, and compromised immune responses.
Metabolomic studies highlight biomarkers such as phenylalanine and choline.
Lipidomic studies uncover altered phospholipids, sphingolipids, and eicosanoids, with lysophosphatidylcholine and ceramide as key biomarkers.
Epigenomic studies show the role of DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.

gap_signals.text: Integrating these omics technologies advances our understanding of ARDS pathophysiology, enabling precision medicine approaches.

important_claims.claim: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants associated with ARDS susceptibility, offering potential therapeutic targets.
important_claims.evidence: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants such as polymorphisms in genes encoding angiotensin-converting enzyme, surfactant proteins, toll-like receptor 4, interleukin-6, Fas/FasL, and vascular endothelial growth factor, offering potential therapeutic targets.

important_claims.claim: Transcriptomic and proteomic studies reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis.
important_claims.evidence: Transcriptomic and proteomic reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis, including dysregulated inflammatory signaling, epithelial and endothelial barrier dysfunction, and compromised immune responses.

important_claims.claim: Metabolomics has highlighted biomarkers, such as phenylalanine and choline, aiding in severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction.
important_claims.evidence: Metabolomics has highlighted biomarkers, such as phenylalanine and choline, aiding in severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction.

important_claims.claim: Lipidomics has uncovered disruptions in lipid metabolism, including altered phospholipids, sphingolipids, and eicosanoids, with key lipid species such as lysophosphatidylcholine and ceramide emerging as biomarkers for severity and outcomes.
important_claims.evidence: Lipidomics has uncovered disruptions in lipid metabolism, including altered phospholipids, sphingolipids, and eicosanoids, with key lipid species such as lysophosphatidylcholine and ceramide emerging as biomarkers for severity and outcomes.

important_claims.claim: Epigenomics explores DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs, revealing their role in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.
important_claims.evidence: Epigenomics explores DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs, revealing their role in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS. These epigenetic changes hold promise for biomarker discovery and personalized therapy.

steps.claim: Omics technologies provide deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms.
steps.evidence: Recent advances in omics technologies, including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, lipidomics, and epigenomics, have provided deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms.

steps.claim: These insights enable the identification of potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers.
steps.evidence: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants associated with ARDS susceptibility, offering potential therapeutic targets. Transcriptomic and proteomic studies reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis. Metabolomics and lipidomics have highlighted biomarkers for severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction. Epigenomics reveals the role of epigenetic changes in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.

causal_chains.outcome: Precision medicine approaches and the development of personalized diagnostics and therapeutic strategies are enabled.
observables: Identification of genetic variants and biomarkers
interventions: Use of omics technologies in ARDS research

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The structured overview of omics technologies in ARDS can guide future research on integrating these technologies for a more comprehensive understanding of ARDS, leading to better diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

keynotes.title: Transforming medicine through pharmacogenomics: Current status, challenges, and emerging horizons

keynote.title: Transforming medicine through pharmacogenomics: Current status, challenges, and emerging horizons
keynote.core_problem: Understanding and addressing the genetic differences that influence a person’s response to medications to improve drug effectiveness and reduce adverse drug reactions (ADRs).
contributions: Highlighting the mechanistic basis of pharmacogenomics, including genetic variability in pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and immunologic responses.
Illustrating key examples such as CYP450 polymorphisms, VKORC1 variants, and human leukocyte antigen alleles to show how genetic differences affect drug metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity.
Discussing the implementation of pharmacogenomics in therapeutic strategies across various medical fields, enabling genotype-guided treatments.
Reviewing global initiatives and successful models of system-wide adoption in countries like the USA, the UK, and the Netherlands.
Identifying challenges and the need for national policy support, public–private partnerships, and low-cost genotyping technologies in India.
experiments_or_evidence: Key examples of genetic differences affecting drug metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity, such as CYP450 polymorphisms, VKORC1 variants, and human leukocyte antigen alleles.
assumptions: Genetic differences significantly influence drug response and can be leveraged to tailor therapy.
limitations: High setup costs for implementing pharmacogenomics.
Limited clinician awareness of pharmacogenomics.
Ethical concerns surrounding genetic discrimination and data privacy.

gap_signals.text: Ethical concerns surrounding genetic discrimination and data privacy.
High setup costs for implementing pharmacogenomics.
Limited clinician awareness of pharmacogenomics.
Limited clinician awareness of pharmacogenomics hinders widespread implementation.
In India, infrastructure remains limited, and there is a need for national policy support and low-cost genotyping technologies.

important_claims.claim: Pharmacogenomics can improve drug effectiveness and reduce ADRs by tailoring therapy based on genetic differences.
important_claims.evidence: Key examples such as CYP450 polymorphisms, VKORC1 variants, and human leukocyte antigen alleles illustrate how genetic differences affect drug metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity.

important_claims.claim: Widespread implementation of pharmacogenomics faces challenges such as high setup costs, limited clinician awareness, and ethical concerns.
important_claims.evidence: Despite the benefits, including reduced ADRs, improved treatment outcomes, and cost savings, these challenges are significant barriers to adoption.

causal_chains.trigger: Understanding genetic differences in drug response

steps.claim: Clinicians can tailor therapy to individual genetic profiles.
steps.evidence: By understanding genetic influences, clinicians can improve drug effectiveness and reduce ADRs.

causal_chains.outcome: Improved treatment outcomes and reduced ADRs

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a comprehensive overview of the current status, challenges, and future directions of pharmacogenomics, which can serve as a foundational reference for researchers and policymakers. It highlights the importance of addressing infrastructure, education, and ethical issues to facilitate the widespread adoption of pharmacogenomics.

keynotes.title: Recent advances in CRISPR-based functional genomics for the study of disease-associated genetic variants

keynote.title: Recent advances in CRISPR-based functional genomics for the study of disease-associated genetic variants

keynotes.title: Precision anesthesia and pharmacogenomics: a scoping review of personalized drug response

keynote.title: Precision anesthesia and pharmacogenomics: a scoping review of personalized drug response
keynote.core_problem: Anesthetic agent selection and dosing have historically relied on empirical models without considering inter-individual variability, leading to adverse drug reactions (ADRs).
contributions: A scoping review synthesizing literature from 2015 to 2025 on genetic variants affecting anesthetic outcomes.
Providing actionable insights for personalized drug selection and dose adjustment.
methods: Systematic database searches and AI-powered tools to identify extensively studied genetic variants impacting the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of common perioperative medications.

gap_signals.text: The need for more research on the integration of pharmacogenomics in anesthesiology to optimize drug selection and dosing.

important_claims.claim: Key genetic variants in metabolic enzymes, transporters, and receptors significantly influence anesthetic outcomes.
important_claims.evidence: Variations in CYP3A4/5 and POR alter midazolam metabolism, risking prolonged sedation. CYP2B6*6 is associated with decreased clearance of propofol and ketamine. BChE deficiency causes significantly prolonged paralysis with succinylcholine. OPRM1 118 A > G (G-allele) carriers show a blunted response to morphine, requiring higher doses. CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolizers (UMs) can have reduced efficacy of ondansetron and risk toxicity from pro-drugs like codeine and tramadol. Pathogenic mutations in RYR1 and CACNA1S identify patients susceptible to Malignant Hyperthermia from volatile anesthetics.

important_claims.claim: PGx holds transformative potential for the field of anesthesiology by offering actionable insights for drug selection and dose adjustment to mitigate ADRs and optimize pain control.
important_claims.evidence: Synthesized literature on genetic variants affecting anesthetic outcomes, providing actionable insights for personalized drug selection and dose adjustment.

keynote.title: Irinotecan toxicity during treatment of metastatic colorectal cancer: focus on pharmacogenomics and personalized medicine
keynote.core_problem: Irinotecan, a drug used in the treatment of advanced or metastatic colorectal cancer, often causes severe toxicities such as neutropenia and diarrhea, which can lead to treatment interruption or cessation, thereby affecting patient prognosis and quality of life.
contributions: Highlighting the role of genetic polymorphisms in enzymes and transporters involved in irinotecan metabolism and excretion, which can result in increased systemic levels of SN-38 (the active metabolite) and higher toxicity.
assumptions: Genetic variations in UGT1A1 and ABC transporters are significant contributors to the variability in irinotecan toxicity among patients.
limitations: The full text does not provide specific details about the limitations, but it is implied that current research is still in the process of implementing genetic screenings into clinical practice.

important_claims.claim: Genetic polymorphisms in UGT1A1 and ABC transporters may result in increased systemic SN-38 levels, directly correlating with toxicity.
important_claims.evidence: Further, ABC transporters (i.e. ABCB1, ABCC1–ABCC6, and ABCG2) are responsible for drug efflux into bile and urine whereas OATP transporters (SLCO1B1) enable its influx from blood into hepatocytes. Genetic polymorphisms in these enzymes/pumps may result in increased systemic SN-38 level, directly correlating with toxicity.

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of genetic polymorphisms in UGT1A1 and ABC transporters

steps.claim: Increased systemic SN-38 levels due to impaired detoxification and efflux

keynotes.title: Abstract C018: Clinical evaluation of a high-content functional precision medicine platform for predicting immunotherapy combination response in solid and hematological cancers

keynote.title: Clinical evaluation of a high-content functional precision medicine platform for predicting immunotherapy combination response in solid and hematological cancers
keynote.core_problem: Response variability to immunotherapies and the lack of predictive biomarkers limit their broader impact in cancer care.
contributions: Development of Optim.AI™ 2.0, which integrates high-content imaging with tumor–immune co-cultures to enable ex vivo assessment of immunotherapy responses.
Evaluation of the platform's ability to predict responses to immunotherapy combinations across solid and hematologic tumors.
methods: Isolation and fluorescent labeling of fresh peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) and tumor cells from non-Hodgkin lymphomas and gynecologic or gastrointestinal cancers.
Testing orthogonal array composite design drug combinations in 384-well immune–cancer co-culture models.
Using high-content imaging of tumor-specific cell death as a quantitative phenotypic readout.
Hybrid experimental–analytical approach to rank all clinically actionable treatments within a 12-drug panel.
experiments_or_evidence: High-content imaging quantified tumor-specific cytotoxicity and visualized key immune–tumor interactions, including immune cell migration and infiltration.

The platform demonstrated feasibility and specificity across cancer types by detecting antigen-dependent responses and distinguishing context-specific antibody-based combinations in both solid and hematologic samples.

Optim.AI™ 2.0 accurately predicted sensitivity and resistance to first-line rituximab in treatment-naïve and relapsed/refractory DLBCL, while highlighting novel immunotherapy combinations with potential benefit in later treatment lines.
assumptions: The ex vivo data generated by Optim.AI™ 2.0 can complement standard clinical and molecular diagnostics.
limitations: Further clinical validation is needed to fully establish the platform's utility in guiding personalized immunotherapy selection.

gap_signals.text: Further clinical validation is needed to fully establish the platform's utility in guiding personalized immunotherapy selection.

With further clinical validation, this platform has the potential to guide personalized immunotherapy selection and, when integrated with molecular profiling, to reveal novel biomarker-informed combinations for defined patient subsets.

important_claims.claim: Optim.AI™ 2.0 enables high-content functional evaluation of immunotherapy combinations in patient-derived tumor-immune co-cultures, generating actionable ex vivo data that can complement standard clinical and molecular diagnostics.
important_claims.evidence: The platform demonstrated feasibility and specificity across cancer types by detecting antigen-dependent responses and distinguishing context-specific antibody-based combinations in both solid and hematologic samples.

important_claims.evidence: Predicted immunotherapy responses were compared with treatment histories to assess clinical concordance.

steps.evidence: The platform uses a hybrid experimental–analytical approach that searches 531,441 permutations derived from 155 ex vivo test combinations to rank all clinically actionable treatments within a 12-drug panel.

keynotes.title: Abstract 494: Multi-omics patient-derived organoid embeddings predict targeted therapy response and KRAS inhibitor sensitivity

keynote.title: Multi-omics patient-derived organoid embeddings predict targeted therapy response and KRAS inhibitor sensitivity
keynote.core_problem: Clinical trials are a major bottleneck in drug development, and the use of patient-derived organoids (PDOs) to derisk clinical trials has been limited by small cohort sizes and lack of systematic validation.
contributions: Demonstrated the use of multi-omics data from a large cohort of PDOs to generate predictive models for drug response and sensitivity to targeted therapies, particularly KRAS inhibitors.
Developed embeddings that predict ex vivo drug response and highlight molecular contexts associated with sensitivity to targeted agents.
methods: Used a large cohort of matched PDOs, longitudinal clinical data, transcriptomic data, and whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data to generate multi-modal patient representations.
Constructed embeddings from omics features to predict drug AUC values and identify biomarkers and pathway-level signatures associated with response to different KRAS-targeted treatments.
experiments_or_evidence: Generated multi-modal patient representations using a large, clinically annotated PDO collection and multi-omics profiling.
Evaluated how these representations can be used to characterize drug response phenotypes and map tumor-intrinsic features to sensitivity to targeted therapies.

gap_signals.text: The impact of PDOs on derisking clinical trials has been limited by small cohort sizes and a lack of systematic validation.

datasets_or_materials: Largest cohort of matched PDOs, longitudinal clinical data, transcriptomic data, and WGS data

important_claims.claim: Large, clinically annotated PDO collections coupled with multi-omics profiling support the construction of embeddings that both predict ex vivo drug response and highlight molecular contexts associated with sensitivity to targeted agents.
important_claims.evidence: Integrating these predictive models with systematic pathway interpretation provides a rigorous framework to uncover transcriptomic and genomic markers of response and resistance.

important_claims.claim: Embeddings from omics features enable accurate prediction of drug AUC values, allowing in silico estimation of PDO drug sensitivity from baseline molecular profiles.
important_claims.evidence: Identification of biomarkers and pathway-level signatures associated with response to different KRAS-targeted treatments.

steps.evidence: Using the largest cohort of matched PDOs, longitudinal clinical data, transcriptomic data, and WGS data, we generate multi-modal patient representations.

steps.claim: Construction of embeddings that predict ex vivo drug response
steps.evidence: Embeddings from omics features enable accurate prediction of drug AUC values, allowing in silico estimation of PDO drug sensitivity from baseline molecular profiles.

steps.claim: Highlighting molecular contexts associated with sensitivity to targeted agents
steps.evidence: Integrating these predictive models with systematic pathway interpretation provides a rigorous framework to uncover transcriptomic and genomic markers of response and resistance.

causal_chains.outcome: Improved ability to predict drug response and sensitivity to targeted therapies, particularly KRAS inhibitors, and to inform patient stratification and trial design.
observables: Predictive accuracy of drug AUC values
Biomarkers and pathway-level signatures associated with response to KRAS-targeted treatments
interventions: Use of large, clinically annotated PDO collections
Integration of multi-omics data and AI-driven analysis

keynotes.title: Circulating Tumor DNA Genomics Reveal Potential Mechanisms of Resistance to BRAF-Targeted Therapies in Patients with BRAF-Mutant Metastatic Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer

keynote.title: Circulating Tumor DNA Genomics Reveal Potential Mechanisms of Resistance to BRAF-Targeted Therapies in Patients with BRAF-Mutant Metastatic Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer
keynote.core_problem: Limited knowledge on the molecular profile of patients with BRAF-mutant NSCLC who progress under BRAF-targeted therapies (BRAF-TT) hampers the development of subsequent therapeutic strategies.
contributions: Evaluation of the clinical utility of ctDNA-targeted sequencing to identify canonical BRAF mutations and genomic alterations potentially related to resistance to BRAF-TT in a large cohort of patients with BRAF-mutant NSCLC.
methods: Prospective study involving 78 patients with advanced BRAF-mutant NSCLC across 27 centers in France.
Collection of 208 blood samples from BRAF-TT-naïve, nonprogressive, and disease progression (PD) patients.
ctDNA sequencing using InVisionFirst-Lung.
In silico structural modeling to predict the functional effect of alterations found in ctDNA.
experiments_or_evidence: Detection of BRAFV600E ctDNA in 74% of BRAF-TT-naïve patients.
Identification of alterations in genes related to the MAPK and PI3K pathways, signal transducers, and protein kinases in 29% of the samples.
Association of ctDNA positivity at the first radiographic evaluation under treatment and BRAF-mutant ctDNA positivity at PD with poor survival.
Identification of potential drivers of resistance in 46% of patients, including activating mutations in effectors of the MAPK and PI3K pathways, as well as alterations in U2AF1, IDH1, and CTNNB1.
assumptions: The presence of specific genetic alterations in ctDNA can be indicative of mechanisms of resistance to BRAF-TT.

gap_signals.text: Limited understanding of the molecular profile of patients with BRAF-mutant NSCLC who progress under BRAF-TT.

datasets_or_materials: Blood samples (n = 208) from 78 patients with advanced BRAF-mutant NSCLC.

important_claims.claim: ctDNA sequencing is clinically relevant for detecting BRAF-activating mutations and identifying alterations potentially related to resistance to BRAF-TT in BRAF-mutant NSCLC.
important_claims.evidence: BRAFV600E ctDNA was detected in 74% of BRAF-TT-naïve patients, and alterations in genes related to the MAPK and PI3K pathways were identified in 29% of the samples. Potential drivers of resistance were identified in 46% of patients.

important_claims.claim: ctDNA positivity at the first radiographic evaluation under treatment and BRAF-mutant ctDNA positivity at PD are associated with poor survival.
important_claims.evidence: Patients with positive ctDNA at the first radiographic evaluation and at PD had worse survival outcomes.

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of BRAF-activating mutations and other genomic alterations in ctDNA

steps.claim: BRAF-activating mutations and other genomic alterations are detected in ctDNA.
steps.evidence: BRAFV600E ctDNA was detected in 74% of BRAF-TT-naïve patients, and alterations in genes related to the MAPK and PI3K pathways were identified in 29% of the samples.

steps.claim: These alterations may lead to resistance to BRAF-TT.
steps.evidence: Potential drivers of resistance were identified in 46% of patients, including activating mutations in effectors of the MAPK and PI3K pathways, as well as alterations in U2AF1, IDH1, and CTNNB1.

causal_chains.outcome: Resistance to BRAF-TT in BRAF-mutant NSCLC
observables: ctDNA positivity at the first radiographic evaluation under treatment

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a framework for using ctDNA genomics to identify and understand mechanisms of resistance to BRAF-TT in BRAF-mutant NSCLC, which can guide the development of new therapeutic strategies.

keynotes.title: Abstract B044: Deep learning-based integration of tumor omics and functional drug screening for precision treatment selection in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors

keynote.title: Deep learning-based integration of tumor omics and functional drug screening for precision treatment selection in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors
keynote.core_problem: The challenge of selecting effective targeted anticancer agents, especially in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors, due to the lack of actionable molecular alterations and poor predictive power of individual biomarkers.
contributions: A unified computational/experimental approach that integrates tumor omic and functional profiling with a deep learning (DL) framework, ScreenDL, for predicting drug responses in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors.
methods: General-purpose pretraining on large-scale cell line pharmaco-omic screens
Fine-tuning on a newly generated pharmaco-omic dataset from >100 patient-derived xenograft (PDX)-derived organoid (PDxO) models
Patient-specific fine-tuning using the ScreenAhead module, which integrates a tumor’s transcriptomic and functional characteristics with prior knowledge
experiments_or_evidence: ScreenDL outperforms existing DL models in never-before-seen PDxOs, achieving a median Pearson correlation (PCC) of 0.32 compared to 0.20 for the next-best model
After incorporating each PDxO’s response to just 12 drugs, ScreenDL provides high-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for 49 out of 95 drugs, with a median PCC of 0.59 across all 95 drugs
ScreenDL outperformed biomarker-only predictors for talazoparib and capivasertib, two targeted agents for specific mutations
In a retrospective preclinical pilot of 11 PDX models, the end-to-end treatment selection strategy achieved a 100% clinical benefit rate and a 65% objective response rate

gap_signals.text: Few data-driven tools exist to inform chemotherapy selection, especially for drugs lacking validated predictive biomarkers in tumors with or without actionable molecular alterations.

code_or_implementation: ScreenDL: A deep learning-based cancer drug response prediction framework
ScreenAhead: A module for patient-specific fine-tuning

important_claims.claim: ScreenDL outperforms existing DL models in predicting drug responses in never-before-seen PDxOs
important_claims.evidence: Achieving a median Pearson correlation (PCC) between observed and predicted response across drugs of 0.32 compared to 0.20 for the next-best tested model

important_claims.claim: Personalization with a small pre-screening panel improves predictions for a broader space of therapies
important_claims.evidence: After incorporating each PDxO’s response to just 12 drugs through PDxO-specific fine-tuning with ScreenAhead, ScreenDL provides high-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for 49 out of 95 drugs, with a median PCC across all 95 drugs of 0.59

important_claims.claim: The end-to-end treatment selection strategy has significant clinical potential
important_claims.evidence: In a retrospective preclinical pilot of 11 PDX models, the strategy achieved a 100% clinical benefit rate and a 65% objective response rate

causal_chains.trigger: Integration of tumor omic and functional profiling with deep learning

steps.claim: Improved drug response prediction
steps.evidence: ScreenDL outperforms existing DL models in never-before-seen PDxOs, achieving a median Pearson correlation (PCC) of 0.32 compared to 00.20 for the next-best model

steps.claim: High-confidence predictions for a broader range of drugs
steps.evidence: After incorporating each PDxO’s response to just 12 drugs, ScreenDL provides high-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for 49 out of 95 drugs, with a median PCC of 0.59 across all 95 drugs

causal_chains.outcome: Enhanced precision in treatment selection for high-risk and metastatic breast tumors
interventions: Use of ScreenDL and ScreenAhead for drug response prediction

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The unified computational/experimental approach, including the use of ScreenDL and ScreenAhead, can be adapted and applied to other types of cancers and therapeutic areas where there is a need for personalized treatment selection based on integrated omic and functional data.

keynotes.title: Abstract NG08: Dissecting and quantifying pancreatic cancer plasticity using single-cell multiomics, lineage tracing and functional genomics reveals novel mediators of therapy resistance

keynote.title: Dissecting and quantifying pancreatic cancer plasticity using single-cell multiomics, lineage tracing and functional genomics reveals novel mediators of therapy resistance
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the role of epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity (EMP) in driving treatment resistance in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC).
contributions: Development of a single-cell multiomic, functional genomic, and computational framework to study tumor cell plasticity.
Identification of 50 transcription factors (TFs) that influence EMP, including known, understudied, and novel TFs.
Demonstration of how these TFs modulate EMP and their impact on treatment resistance.
methods: Single-cell RNA-seq (scSeq) and ATAC-seq on patient-derived PDAC cell lines and clinical biopsies.
Lineage tracing experiments using an expressed lentiviral barcoding system (ClonMapper).
Generative probabilistic model of lineage tracing data.
Elastic net regression to identify TFs influencing EMP.
CRISPRi perturb-seq experiments to perturb identified TFs and observe effects on cell states and transitions.
experiments_or_evidence: Profiled twelve patient-derived PDAC cell lines by scSeq and identified convergent epithelial and mesenchymal gene programs.
Proved the presence of EMP through lineage tracing experiments, showing a single cell can produce progeny in both epithelial and mesenchymal states.
Developed a generative probabilistic model demonstrating different transition matrices for clones, indicating distinct levels of plasticity.
Identified 50 TFs whose expression significantly explained the propensity for EMP over time across clonal populations.
Used CRISPRi perturb-seq to perturb 50 predicted TFs and collected 1.5 million single-cell transcriptomic profiles, finding that 60% of guides had significant perturbation effects.

Treated four barcoded cell lines with FOLFIRINOX or targeted therapy and performed scSeq, finding enrichment of barcodes biased for mesenchymal states and high transition rates after FOLFIRINOX treatment.
Validated the role of proposed plasticity factors in human patients using paired biopsy samples from 23 patients in a phase 2 clinical trial.
assumptions: The observed changes in cell states and transition rates are indicative of true plasticity and not other confounding factors.
The identified TFs play a significant role in regulating EMP and treatment resistance.
limitations: The study is limited to in vitro and in vivo models, and further validation in more extensive clinical settings is needed.
The CRISPRi perturbations may have off-target effects, which could complicate the interpretation of results.

gap_signals.text: The study is limited to in vitro and in vivo models, and further validation in more extensive clinical settings is needed.
The CRISPRi perturbations may have off-target effects, which could complicate the interpretation of results.

Historical methods for measuring plasticity either fail to definitively prove the existence of plasticity, are restricted in measurements outside of native tissues, or are unable to quantify the role of plasticity in treatment resistance.
The relative contribution of intrinsic resistance and plasticity to treatment resistance is still poorly understood.

datasets_or_materials: Three PDAC cell lines used for lineage tracing experiments
Paired biopsy samples from 23 patients in a phase 2 clinical trial
code_or_implementation: Generative probabilistic model of lineage tracing data
Elastic net regression for identifying TFs
Negative binomial regression for estimating effect sizes of guide RNAs
Supervised Bayesian matrix factorization approach (Spectra) for learning gene programs

important_claims.claim: A single cell can produce progeny in both epithelial and mesenchymal states, proving the presence of EMP.
important_claims.evidence: Lineage tracing experiments using ClonMapper and scSeq showed that a single cell can produce progeny in both epithelial and mesenchymal states.

important_claims.claim: Different clones have distinct levels of plasticity, as indicated by different transition matrices.
important_claims.evidence: A generative probabilistic model of lineage tracing data demonstrated that clones had different EMT and MET rates, suggesting distinct levels of plasticity.

important_claims.claim: Several TFs, including Elf3, Sox2, Sox4, Klf3, Klf5, and Atf4, are important regulators of EMP.
important_claims.evidence: Elastic net regression identified 50 TFs, including several known and novel TFs, whose expression significantly explained the propensity for EMP over time across clonal populations.

important_claims.claim: Knockdown of certain TFs, such as Grhl2 and FoxA1, biases cells towards mesenchymal states, while knockdown of others, such as Batf2, Snai1, Rel, Zeb1, Nr2f1, and Sox4, biases cells towards epithelial states.
important_claims.evidence: CRISPRi perturb-seq experiments showed that knockdown of specific TFs influenced cell state transitions, biasing cells towards either mesenchymal or epithelial states.

important_claims.claim: High expression of proposed plasticity factors in baseline samples correlates with a redistribution of states in follow-up biopsies.
important_claims.evidence: Validation in human patients using paired biopsy samples from a phase 2 clinical trial showed that high expression of proposed plasticity factors in baseline samples correlated with a redistribution of states in follow-up biopsies.

steps.claim: Enrichment of barcodes biased for mesenchymal states
steps.evidence: After treatment with FOLFIRINOX, there was an enrichment of barcodes biased for cells in mesenchymal states, consistent with selection against epithelial cells.

steps.claim: Enrichment of barcodes with high transition rates
steps.evidence: Barcodes with the highest inferred state transition rates were also enriched after FOLFIRINOX treatment.

causal_chains.outcome: Selection for mesenchymal states and high plasticity contributes to treatment resistance.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a robust experimental and quantitative framework for studying tumor cell plasticity, identifies key TFs that mediate EMP, and offers a paradigm for using perturbations to homogenize tumor populations towards treatment-sensitive phenotypes, which can be applied to other cancers and therapeutic contexts.

keynotes.title: Revolutionizing Healthcare: AI-Driven Innovations in Drug Development and Personalized Medicine

keynote.title: Revolutionizing Healthcare: AI-Driven Innovations in Drug Development and Personalized Medicine
keynote.core_problem: The need to improve the precision, speed, and efficiency of healthcare solutions, particularly in drug development and personalized medicine, while addressing issues such as algorithmic bias, data privacy, regulatory validation, and the requirement for explainable AI.
contributions: Examination of how AI is transforming drug development, optimization, diagnostics, and patient-specific therapy approaches.
Highlighting recent clinical trials and scientific advances, including virtual clinical simulations, AI-powered diagnostics, and AI-supported COVID-19 drug development.
experiments_or_evidence: AI systems analyze large datasets, including genetic profiles, electronic medical records, and empirical data, to predict drug responses, identify new treatment targets, and reduce side effects.
Use of tools like AlphaFold and Deep Docking for protein structure prediction and molecular interaction modeling.
IBM Watson and in silico medicine are used to improve clinical decision-making and predictive analytics.
limitations: Algorithmic bias, data privacy, regulatory validation, and the need for explainable AI are significant challenges that must be addressed.

Tools like AlphaFold and Deep Docking lead in protein structure prediction and molecular interaction modeling, while IBM Watson and in silico medicine improve clinical decision-making and predictive analytics.

keynotes.title: MES in Pharmaceutical Manufacturing: Enabling the Future of Personalized Medicine

keynote.title: MES in Pharmaceutical Manufacturing: Enabling the Future of Personalized Medicine
keynote.core_problem: The transition from standardized to personalized medicine requires new manufacturing processes that can handle individualized therapies, which are tailored to patients' genetic profiles and specific needs.
contributions: Highlighting the role of MES in enabling personalized medicine through streamlined batch management, flexible manufacturing, real-time process control, and improved supply chain operations.

gap_signals.text: Manufacturing processes must evolve to meet the unique challenges of personalized medicine production.

MES further ensures regulatory compliance through automated documentation and embedded quality control while supporting integration with diagnostic technologies to translate patient data into precise manufacturing parameters. Additionally, MES optimizes supply chain operations by improving material tracking, minimizing waste, and enabling efficient distribution of time-sensitive treatments.

keynotes.title: From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing.

keynote.title: From print settings to printlet performance: a study on critical quality attributes in pellet-based direct extrusion additive manufacturing
keynote.core_problem: Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) requires filaments with specific mechanical and physical properties, limiting formulation flexibility. Pellets could be used as feedstock material for direct extrusion additive manufacturing (DEAM) to address this limitation.
contributions: Explores the potential of pellet-based DEAM through a design-of-experiments approach using a formulation unsuitable for FFF.
Evaluates the effects of process parameters during twin-screw extrusion on various pellet characteristics.
Identifies the extrusion multiplier as a new key process parameter enabling personalized medicine.
methods: Design-of-experiments approach to evaluate the impact of process parameters on pellet characteristics.
Full factorial screening design to evaluate the impact of four printing parameters on critical quality attributes of 3D printed tablets.
experiments_or_evidence: Brittle filaments containing 60% anhydrous theophylline and 40% Soluplus® (m/m) were prepared via hot melt extrusion and shredded into pellets.
The study evaluated the effects of screw speed, barrel and die temperature on pellet characteristics such as content uniformity, API degradation, shape, size, flowability, and friability.

A full factorial screening design was used to assess the impact of print temperature, infill, overlap, and extrusion multiplier on tablet mass, content uniformity, drug degradation, mechanical properties, dissolution, dimensions, and porosity.
assumptions: Pellets can be used as feedstock material for DEAM, potentially offering more flexibility than FFF.

gap_signals.text: Pellet-based DEAM is a promising approach for manufacturing oral solid dosage forms with versatile properties and fewer material constraints compared to FFF.

datasets_or_materials: Brittle filaments containing 60% anhydrous theophylline and 40% Soluplus® (m/m)

important_claims.claim: Die temperature exerted the most significant impact on the friability and visual quality of the pellets.

important_claims.evidence: A full factorial screening design evaluated the impact of four printing parameters (print temperature, infill, overlap and extrusion multiplier) on critical quality attributes of the 3D printed tablets, including tablet mass, content uniformity, drug degradation, mechanical properties, dissolution, dimensions, and porosity.

important_claims.evidence: The tensile strength was mainly influenced by infill, and the tablet brittleness index was a reliable indication of the brittleness of the printlets.

important_claims.evidence: Porosity was correlated with in vitro dissolution, both influenced by infill and extrusion multiplier.

objective_decomposition.objective: 在定义明确的患者群体、疾病类型和治疗类别中，个体的遗传变异、肿瘤或疾病分子表型、器官功能、免疫状态、既往治疗史以及制造过程参数，是否能够以可重复、可校准、因果上可解释的方式预测：1. 某一治疗方案的药代动力学暴露、毒性和适宜剂量；2. 某一靶向药物、免疫治疗、细胞治疗或核酸药物的临床获益与耐药风险；3. 个体化制剂或自体细胞产品能否在身份、纯度、效力、无菌性、批间一致性、周转时间和成本约束下稳定制造；4. 上述预测在不同祖源、年龄、肝肾功能、疾病分期、医疗中心和检测平台迁移时是否仍成立。禁止将统计相关、患者画像匹配或模型准确率直接解释为因果治疗获益；禁止将实验室样本、类器官、动物模型或单中心回顾性结果直接外推为临床个体化治疗有效；禁止将技术尚不成熟误判为生物学上绝对不可行。执行完整闭环：Boxue → Objective Decomposition → ZhiZhi → TanXi → Socrates → MingLi → YanZhen → DuZhi → BianLun → MingLi 修订 → YanZhen 复审。
objective_decomposition.domain: Personalized Medicine / Precision Medicine / Pharmacogenomics / Cancer Genomics / Clinical Pharmacology / Multi-omics / Patient-derived Models / Cell and Gene Therapy Manufacturing / Biostatistics / Regulatory Science
objective_decomposition.research_brief:
创建并运行一个 AutoGen 科研闭环验证项目。

  项目标题：
  Can we design and manufacture medicines customized for individual people?
  从个体分子表型、剂量响应到可制造性的精准医疗因果证据闭环验证

  领域：
  Personalized Medicine / Precision Medicine / Pharmacogenomics / Cancer Genomics /
  Clinical Pharmacology / Multi-omics / Patient-derived Models /
  Cell and Gene Therapy Manufacturing / Biostatistics / Regulatory Science

  研究目标：
  不要泛泛讨论 精准医疗将改变未来医疗。围绕以下可分解、可证伪的科学与转化问题开展验证：

  在定义明确的患者群体、疾病类型和治疗类别中，个体的遗传变异、肿瘤或疾病分子表型、
  器官功能、免疫状态、既往治疗史以及制造过程参数，是否能够以可重复、可校准、
  因果上可解释的方式预测：

  1. 某一治疗方案的药代动力学暴露、毒性和适宜剂量；
  2. 某一靶向药物、免疫治疗、细胞治疗或核酸药物的临床获益与耐药风险；
  3. 个体化制剂或自体细胞产品能否在身份、纯度、效力、无菌性、批间一致性、
     周转时间和成本约束下稳定制造；
  4. 上述预测在不同祖源、年龄、肝肾功能、疾病分期、医疗中心和检测平台迁移时是否仍成立。

  禁止将统计相关、患者画像匹配或模型准确率直接解释为因果治疗获益；
  禁止将实验室样本、类器官、动物模型或单中心回顾性结果直接外推为临床个体化治疗有效；
  禁止将技术尚不成熟误判为生物学上绝对不可行。

  执行完整闭环：
  Boxue → Objective Decomposition → ZhiZhi → TanXi → Socrates →
  MingLi → YanZhen → DuZhi → BianLun → MingLi 修订 → YanZhen 复审。


  ## Boxue：Objective Decomposition

  在任何宽泛检索前，先运行 decompose_research_objective。
  输出不是一组研究方向，而是独立的、可被推翻的子假设。

 

  ## ZhiZhi：文献与证据知识库要求

  数据源与质量边界

  - 主要使用 semantic_scholar、PubMed、arXiv；临床前沿预印本可补充 medRxiv/bioRxiv，但必须明确其未经同行评审状态。
  - 分层导入至少 15 篇经质量筛选的文献；数量不是目标，必须覆盖每个已选子假设的证据窗口。
  - 对摘要缺失、unknown 字段、来源可疑或 requires_human_review 的记录，执行提取修复、降权或停留在背景层，不得作为机制合
    同的唯一证据。

  - 不得制造论文、临床试验、监管文件、效应量、患者结局或指南推荐。

  P0–P4 分层

  P0 / L3：近期预印本
    - 精准医疗、临床药理、多组学、患者来源模型、个体化细胞/核酸制造
    - 要求标注未经同行评审；检索失败必须显式标记证据不足

  P1 / L2：近2年高质量原始研究
    - 前瞻性临床队列、随机或准实验研究
    - 外部验证、临床药理、真实世界目标试验模拟
    - 多中心临床/转化研究和受控制造研究

  P2 / L4：机制、实验与理论证据
    - 酶动力学、PK/PD、药物转运、受体占有
    - 单细胞、多组学、功能测定、类器官
    - CMC、效力测定、放行标准、过程分析技术

  P3 / L0：系统综述、临床指南、监管和方法学综述
    - 药物基因组学实施指南
    - 精准肿瘤学、患者来源模型和先进治疗产品监管综述
    - 偏倚、因果推断、临床预测模型报告规范

  P4 / L1：奠基研究与历史脉络
    - 关键药物基因组学机制发现
    - 个体化靶向治疗或细胞治疗的奠基性临床/制造研究
    - 仅用于历史和机制脉络，不得替代当前外部验证

  必须覆盖的子空间

  - 药物基因组学、代谢酶、转运体、HLA 风险、PK/PD 与剂量优化；
  - 肿瘤或疾病基因组、转录组、蛋白组、单细胞和空间组学；
  - 预测性标志物与预后性标志物的因果区分；
  - 真实世界数据、目标试验模拟、选择偏倚、祖源偏倚和检测平台漂移；
  - 患者来源类器官、PDX、功能测定与临床结局之间的迁移误差；
  - 自体细胞、核酸药物或个体化制剂的 CMC、身份链、效力、无菌、放行和供应链；
  - 监管、知情同意、数据治理、隐私、算法审计和可及性；
  - 经济性：周转时间、失败制造批次、支付方式和中心间可复制性。

  必须排除或降权的内容

  - 只报告模型 AUC、没有临床终点或外部验证的纯算法论文；
  - 只有疾病关联、没有治疗策略或可观测中介的关联研究；
  - 与精准医疗无关的普通生物信息学、一般医院调度或常规药物合成论文；
  - 仅动物或细胞系研究、却声称直接预测患者获益的论文；
  - 没有来源、无明确样本定义、混合多个治疗场景且不可拆分的营销报告。

  DeepSurvey 风格知识构建

  每篇入库论文除 method-scenario-benchmark 外，必须提取：

  keynote:
    core_problem: 该论文解决的具体临床或制造问题
    patient_population: 人群、适应证、入排标准和治疗场景
    causal_chains:
      - trigger: 干预、遗传变异、制造参数或自然实验条件
        steps:
          - claim: 中介机制
            evidence: 论文中的来源摘录
            evidence_type: experimental | theoretical | observational | inferred
        outcome: 临床、药代、功能或制造终点
        observables: 可直接测量的信号
        interventions: 可操纵变量
    experimental_evidence: 样本数、测量条件、对照和主要终点
    assumptions: 隐含假设、可迁移条件和未测混杂
    limitations: 作者明确局限
    important_claims: 声明与证据的一一对应
    future_directions: 明确未来工作
    code_or_implementation: 公开代码、模型版本、分析环境；不得下载或处理患者敏感数据

  ## TanXi：Gap 探索要求

  TanXi 首先做因果断链检测，而不是把方法尚未用于某场景作为高价值 Gap。

  优先发现以下问题：

  1. 遗传变异 → 功能表型 → 药物暴露 → 毒性/获益 中，哪一段缺乏可测量中介、干预证据或外部验证；
  2. 某些生物标志物究竟预测治疗效应，还是仅反映预后、疾病负荷或医生选择治疗的偏倚；
  3. 单次基线多组学预测与治疗过程中克隆演化、耐药、免疫状态变化之间的断链；
  4. 类器官、PDX 或功能检测的体外响应与临床响应不一致的机制：采样偏倚、微环境缺失、时间滞后还是剂量暴露差异；
  5. 个体化细胞/核酸产品中，起始材料、过程参数、关键质量属性、效力和临床可给药性之间是否存在未验证的因果链；
  6. 不同祖源、年龄、肝肾功能、合并用药、检测平台和医疗中心之间，预测规则是否出现校准失效；
  7. 预测性能改进是否被制造周期、失败率、成本和可及性抵消；
  8. 监管/标准体系是否无法覆盖个体化产品的身份链、效力检测和持续工艺变更。

  每个 ranked gap 必须输出：

  mechanism_draft:
    input: 已定义的干预、患者分层或制造变量
    proposed_mediator: 仅可填写文献来源支持的中介；否则保持 unresolved
    output: 明确临床、药代、功能或制造终点
    source_clue: 原文 citation 与 excerpt
    causal_gap:
      missing_kind: intermediate_mechanism | observability | intervention |
                    counterfactual | evidence_window
      evidence_needed: 下一步需要何种直接证据
    sub_hypothesis_id: SH1 | SH2 | SH3 | SH4
    unresolved_fields:
      - identity
      - location_or_scope
      - dynamics
      - reversibility
      - observability
      - intervention
      - counterfactual

  禁止把某 AI 方法尚未用于某癌种某测序技术还没和某药物配对直接列为核心 Gap。

  ## Socrates：机制合同与因果补全

  只选择排名第一、且属于机制型或矛盾型的 Gap 建立机制合同。

  Socrates evidence contract:
    identity: 中介实体/过程到底是什么
    location_or_scope: 位于哪类细胞、组织、患者分层、制造环节或时间窗
    dynamics: 如何随剂量、治疗周期、病程或制造批次变化
    reversibility: 是否可逆，何种条件下可恢复
    observability: 如何直接测量，而不是只测临床终点
    intervention: 什么变量可在伦理和技术上被操纵
    counterfactual: 若抑制/移除中介，预期发生什么

  每个字段必须有：

  evidence:
    - citation: 真实论文、试验、指南或受控制造来源
      excerpt: 支持该字段的原文摘录
      paper_id: PaperGraph记录
      evidence_type: experimental | theoretical | observational | inferred

  Socrates 必须同时输出：

  - 反事实或准实验设计；
  - 第一性原理推导待补项，例如酶动力学、药物暴露—反应关系、受体占有、细胞扩增动力学、质量属性—效力关系；
  - 竞争机制消除计划；
  - 哪些证据来自人体、体外系统、动物模型或制造过程，禁止混淆证据层级。

  若 verdict != COMPLETE，输出 INSUFFICIENT_EVIDENCE、剩余字段和定向检索建议，停止 MingLi 假设生成；不得用常识、临床直觉
  或模板补全。

  ## MingLi：假设生成要求

  MingLi 只可消费：

  - Socrates COMPLETE 的机制合同；
  - 对应子假设满足 P0–P2 证据窗口；
  - 有明确患者范围、治疗范围和可观测终点的 Gap。

  每条假设必须包含：

  hypothesis:
    sub_hypothesis_id: SHx
    patient_scope: 年龄、适应证、疾病阶段、关键入排条件
    treatment_scope: 具体药物/治疗类别/制造平台
    causal_chain: X → Z → Y
    X: 可干预变量或可定义的自然实验
    Z: 直接可测量中介
    Y: 临床、药代、功能或制造终点
    operating_conditions:
      - 给药方案、剂量、治疗周期
      - 肝肾功能、合并用药、样本采集时点
      - 制造工艺、放行窗口或储运条件
    observables:
      - 药物暴露、毒性分级、反应率、无进展生存
      - ctDNA、转录组、蛋白组、免疫功能、类器官功能读数
      - 身份、纯度、效力、无菌、周转时间和失败率
    null_hypothesis: 不存在经中介 Z 传递的治疗效应异质性
    alternative_explanations:
      - 未测临床混杂
      - 预后性而非预测性标志物
      - 检测误差、选择偏倚或时间依赖偏倚
      - 制造批次或中心效应
    falsification_condition: 预注册的失败条件
    source_mapping: 每个因果链步骤对应 Socrates citation/excerpt

  合格假设的形式应类似：

  > 在预先定义的患者分层和治疗方案中，若通过 X 改变或识别个体化治疗条件，并且直接测得中介 Z 的预期变化，而 Y 在匹配对照
  > 下按预注册方向改变，则支持该机制；若 Z 未改变、Y 与 Z 脱钩，或竞争机制更能解释数据，则否定或修订假设。

  禁止使用AI 能改善个体化治疗通过多组学理解患者采用特定技术提升疗效之类没有机制实体和证伪条件的万能模板。

  ## YanZhen：两次机制审计

  初审

  - 内部一致性：变量、患者范围、治疗范围、时间顺序和中介定义是否一致；
  - 数据一致性：citation/excerpt 是否真正支持所声称的人群、干预和终点；
  - 因果一致性：是否把相关误当治疗效应，是否遗漏关键混杂；
  - 工程与生物学区分：制造失败、成本或周转时间限制不能被误判为疗法生物学无效；
  - 输出：unsupported_claims、required_actions、verdict。

  至少两个 regime-shift 检验

  regime_shift_1:
    condition: 祖源、年龄或性别构成发生变化
    question: 遗传风险或治疗预测是否仍校准，是否存在群体公平性失效

  regime_shift_2:
    condition: 肝肾功能、合并用药、疾病分期或治疗线数变化
    question: 剂量—暴露—毒性因果链是否仍成立

  regime_shift_3:
    condition: 检测平台、样本采集时间点、医疗中心或制造设施变化
    question: 生物标志物和质量属性是否可重复，中心效应是否主导结论

  regime_shift_4:
    condition: 从回顾性队列迁移到前瞻性、真实世界或受控临床研究
    question: 是否存在标签泄漏、选择偏倚、时间依赖偏倚或疗效衰减

  若初审不是 PASS，YanZhen 必须把具体失败点反馈给 MingLi 和 DuZhi；不得直接输出成功结论。

  ## DuZhi + BianLun：3–5 轮结构化辩论

  DuZhi 每轮必须针对上一轮的具体回应追问，并至少覆盖：

  1. 个体化的操作定义是什么：是药物选择、剂量、给药时机、组合疗法，还是产品制造参数？
  2. 遗传或多组学标志物如何从关联提升为预测性治疗标志物？是否存在治疗分配偏倚？
  3. 中介变量 Z 如何直接测量？检测误差、批次效应和采样时间是否足以掩盖真实变化？
  4. 若不同中心、不同祖源或不同检测平台中预测效果显著下降，机制是否仍成立？
  5. 若患者来源模型预测有效但临床无效，是模型缺少微环境、药物暴露不匹配，还是临床终点定义错误？
  6. 个体化制造产品的效力、纯度或周转时间改善，是否被制造失败率、成本、可及性或监管限制抵消？
  7. 所谓不能为个体制造药物究竟是生物学限制、临床证据限制、制造限制，还是支付和监管限制？

  BianLun 每轮必须输出：

  round_summary:
    resolved_disputes: []
    unresolved_disputes: []
    causal_links_requiring_revision: []
    evidence_missing: []
    required_mingli_revision: []

  MingLi 必须保留修订前后版本、修改理由、引用变化和仍未解决的不确定性。

  ## 最终交付物与安全边界

  最终输出必须包括：

  - Objective Decomposition、每个 SH 的状态和组合结论门槛；
  - P0–P4 文献覆盖、子空间覆盖、来源质量和预印本缺口报告；
  - PaperGraph 因果证据图与断链位置；
  - TanXi ranked gaps 与 mechanism_draft；
  - Socrates evidence contract、反事实设计、第一性原理待补项；
  - 初始假设、3–5 轮 DuZhi 质询、BianLun 归纳、MingLi 修订轨迹；
  - YanZhen 初审与复审；
  - 最终状态：ACCEPT_FOR_EXPERIMENT、CONDITIONAL_ACCEPT、REJECTED 或 REQUIRES_HUMAN_REVIEW；
  - 可证伪的验证设计，包括患者选择、对照、混杂控制、外部验证、统计分析计划和数据治理要求；
  - 个体化制造路径的 CMC 验证：身份链、关键质量属性、效力、纯度、无菌、批间一致性、周转时间、失败率和成本；
  - 未解决证据缺口、不能外推的人群/治疗范围，以及后续定向检索建议。

  所有涉及真实患者数据、治疗分配、剂量改变、细胞或基因治疗制造的实验，都只能作为研究设计输出；实际执行必须经过 IRB/伦理
  审查、临床试验注册、知情同意、隐私与数据治理审查，以及相应药品监管要求。

sub_hypotheses.source_objective: 在定义明确的患者群体、疾病类型和治疗类别中，个体的遗传变异、肿瘤或疾病分子表型、器官功能、免疫状态、既往治疗史以及制造过程参数，是否能够以可重复、可校准、因果上可解释的方式预测：1. 某一治疗方案的药代动力学暴露、毒性和适宜剂量；2. 某一靶向药物、免疫治疗、细胞治疗或核酸药物的临床获益与耐药风险；3. 个体化制剂或自体细胞产品能否在身份、纯度、效力、无菌性、批间一致性、周转时间和成本约束下稳定制造；4. 上述预测在不同祖源、年龄、肝肾功能、疾病分期、医疗中心和检测平台迁移时是否仍成立。禁止将统计相关、患者画像匹配或模型准确率直接解释为因果治疗获益；禁止将实验室样本、类器官、动物模型或单中心回顾性结果直接外推为临床个体化治疗有效；禁止将技术尚不成熟误判为生物学上绝对不可行。执行完整闭环：Boxue → Objective Decomposition → ZhiZhi → TanXi → Socrates → MingLi → YanZhen → DuZhi → BianLun → MingLi 修订 → YanZhen 复审。

combination_hypothesis.statement: If SH1, SH2, SH3, and SH4 are all supported, then it is possible to design and manufacture medicines customized for individual people in a reproducible, calibrated, and causally interpretable manner.
combination_hypothesis.integration_test: A comprehensive multi-center, multi-ethnic, and multi-platform study that integrates genetic, molecular, and manufacturing data to validate the combined predictive and manufacturing models.

retrieval: Use semantic_scholar, PubMed, arXiv; include medRxiv/bioRxiv for recent preprints. Import at least 15 quality-screened papers covering each sub-hypothesis. Exclude irrelevant or low-quality sources.
gap_detection: Identify gaps in causal chains, observability, and intervention evidence. Prioritize gaps in genetic to functional phenotype, baseline multi-omics to treatment evolution, and in vitro to in vivo translation.
hypothesis: Generate hypotheses based on Socrates COMPLETE mechanism contracts. Ensure clear patient scope, treatment scope, and observable endpoints. Avoid generic claims and ensure falsifiability.
verification: Audit for internal, data, and causal consistency. Perform regime-shift tests for ancestry, age, liver/kidney function, and platform changes. Ensure clear pass/fail conditions and provide detailed revision requirements if not passed.

decomposition_rules: Each sub-hypothesis must expose an intervention, observable outcome, falsification condition, and evidence window.
A combined conclusion is admissible only after the relevant component hypotheses are independently evaluated.
Missing quantitative bounds remain explicitly unresolved; they must not be fabricated from the objective.

edges.evidence_excerpt: Recent advances in omics technologies, including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, lipidomics, and epigenomics, have provided deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms.

edges.citation: L. Al-Husinat et al. (2025) Advancing omics technologies in acute respiratory distress syndrome: paving the way for personalized medicine doi:10.1186/s40635-025-00766-4
edges.evidence_excerpt: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants associated with ARDS susceptibility, offering potential therapeutic targets. Transcriptomic and proteomic studies reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis. Metabolomics and lipidomics have highlighted biomarkers for severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction. Epigenomics reveals the role of epigenetic changes in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.

chains.citation: A. Latini et al. (2019) miRNAs in drug response variability: potential utility as biomarkers for personalized medicine. doi:10.2217/pgs-2019-0089
chains.trigger: Differences in miRNA levels or genetic variants

steps.claim: Regulation of numerous genes involved in the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of drugs is affected.
steps.evidence: Acting on the regulation of numerous genes involved in the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of drugs, differences in the levels of circulating miRNAs or genetic variants in the sequences of the miRNA genes can contribute to interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy.

chains.outcome: Interindividual variability in drug response, both in terms of toxicity and efficacy.
interventions: Measurement of circulating miRNA levels or genetic variants in miRNA genes

chains.citation: L. Al-Husinat et al. (2025) Advancing omics technologies in acute respiratory distress syndrome: paving the way for personalized medicine doi:10.1186/s40635-025-00766-4

steps.claim: Omics technologies provide deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms.
steps.evidence: Recent advances in omics technologies, including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, lipidomics, and epigenomics, have provided deeper insights into ARDS mechanisms.

steps.claim: These insights enable the identification of potential therapeutic targets and biomarkers.
steps.evidence: Genomic studies have identified genetic variants associated with ARDS susceptibility, offering potential therapeutic targets. Transcriptomic and proteomic studies reveal distinct biomarker profiles associated with ARDS pathogenesis. Metabolomics and lipidomics have highlighted biomarkers for severity assessment, subphenotype stratification, and treatment response prediction. Epigenomics reveals the role of epigenetic changes in regulating inflammation, immune responses, and tissue repair in ARDS.

chains.outcome: Precision medicine approaches and the development of personalized diagnostics and therapeutic strategies are enabled.
observables: Identification of genetic variants and biomarkers
interventions: Use of omics technologies in ARDS research

identity.claim: At the crossroads of genomics and pharmacology, pharmacogenomics is revolutionizing healthcare by tailoring drug therapies to individual genetic profiles thereby reducing the risk of adverse drug reactions and propelling the field of precision medicine forward.
identity.citation: Anantha Lakshmi Jakka et al. (2025) From genomics to clinic: the transformative impact of AI in pharmacogenomics and personalized medicine. doi:10.1080/14622416.2025.2591596

evidence.title: From genomics to clinic: the transformative impact of AI in pharmacogenomics and personalized medicine.
evidence.excerpt: This review enunciates the existing applications, translational pathways, and prospects of AI in pharmacogenomics, its promise in achieving the goal of precision medicine ensuring the proper treatment of right patient at the right moment.

dynamics.title: From genomics to clinic: the transformative impact of AI in pharmacogenomics and personalized medicine.

Blood samples (n = 208) were collected from BRAF-TT–naïve patients (n = 47), patients nonprogressive under treatment (n = 115), or patients at disease progression (PD) to BRAF-TT (24/46 on BRAF monotherapy and 22/46 on BRAF/MEK combination therapy).
Full text excerpt:
Experimental Design: This was a prospective study of 78 patients with advanced BRAF-mutant NSCLC, enrolled in 27 centers across France.

references: Arnav Mehta et al. (2024) Abstract NG08: Dissecting and quantifying pancreatic cancer plasticity using single-cell multiomics, lineage tracing and functional genomics reveals novel mediators of therapy resistance doi:10.1158/1538-7445.am2024-ng08

evidence_type_annotations.claim: omics profiling

identification of novel transcription factors (TFs) that mediate epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity (EMP) and influence treatment resistance in PDAC, and a robust experimental and quantitative framework for studying tumor cell plasticity

To study the effect of plasticity in treatment resistance, we treated four barcoded cell lines with the first-line chemotherapy combination FOLFIRINOX (5-fluorouracil, oxaliplatin and SN-38, the active metabolite of irinotecan) or targeted therapy and performed scSeq yielding over 600,00 single-cell...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

Two major mechanisms have been suggested for treatment evasion: (1) the intrinsic resistance of an existing state to a therapy regimen and (2) plasticity of therapy-sensitive states to adopt more resistant states.

While these approaches are observationally consistent with the notion of plasticity, they either fail to definitively prove the existence of plasticity, are restricted in measurements of plasticity outside of native tissues or are unable to quantify the role of plasticity in treatment resistance.

To better understand and quantify the role of EMP in driving treatment resistance of human PDAC, we have developed single-cell multiomic, functional genomic and computational methods applied to patient-derived models and clinical biopsies.

We first profiled twelve patient-derived PDAC cell lines by single-cell RNA-seq (scSeq) and learned convergent epithelial and mesenchymal gene programs that were consistent with programs observed in patient samples.
We next performed lineage tracing experiments in three PDAC cell lines using an expressed lentiviral barcoding system (ClonMapper).
By performing scSeq on these barcoded lines at weekly timepoints over four weeks, we proved the presence of EMP by showing a single cell can produce progeny in both epithelial and mesenchymal states.

references: Dhruv Pratap Singh Jaitawat et al. (2025) Revolutionizing Healthcare: AI-Driven Innovations in Drug Development and Personalized Medicine doi:10.2174/0118756921395626250905121219

evidence_type_annotations.claim: artificial intelligence (AI) systems
AI is transforming the pharmaceutical and personalized medicine industries, improving the precision, speed, and efficiency of healthcare solutions.
issues, including algorithmic bias, data privacy, regulatory validation, and the requirement for explainable AI, still exist
Full text excerpt:

The precision, speed, and efficiency of healthcare solutions are being improved by artificial intelligence (AI), which is drastically changing the pharmaceutical manufacturing and customized medicine industries.
The many ways that AI is transforming drug development, optimization, diagnostics, and patient-specific therapy approaches are examined in this paper.
AI greatly cuts down on development time and expense in pharmaceutical research by enabling structure-based virtual screening, de novo drug discovery, and repurposing of already-approved medications.

While systems like IBM Watson and in silico medicine improve clinical decision-making and predictive analytics, tools like AlphaFold and Deep Docking are leading the way in protein structure prediction and molecular interaction modelling.
Through automation, quality control, and predictive maintenance, AI also improves pharmaceutical production, guaranteeing constant product quality and regulatory compliance.
Notwithstanding its promise, issues, including algorithmic bias, data privacy, regulatory validation, and the requirement for explainable AI, still exist.

Full text excerpt:

It reviews the historical evolution of additive manufacturing in drug production, discusses the technological advancements up to 2018, and examines the current methodologies and statistical trends associated with 3D-printed drug formulations.

Through a detailed literature review, methodological analysis, and statistical evaluation, this study highlights the benefits and challenges of incorporating 3D printing into pharmaceutical manufacturing.
In addition, we present a pilot statistical analysis that compares key parameters in formulation consistency, dosage accuracy, and production scalability.

Our findings suggest that 3D printing offers a promising alternative to conventional manufacturing methods, particularly for patients requiring customized therapies due to genetic variability, comorbidities, or other individualized needs.
